title.en: WebP vs PNG: Which Format Is Better for the Web?

description.en: WebP vs PNG comparison — file size, quality, transparency, browser support, use cases, and when to use each format for web images.

intro.en: WebP and PNG both support lossless compression and transparency, but WebP consistently produces smaller files. WebP lossless images are approximately 26% smaller than equivalent PNGs. However, PNG has effectively universal software support while WebP may not open in older applications.

a.en: Smaller file sizes than PNG (lossless and lossy modes)
Supports both lossless and lossy modes in one format

b.en: Universal software compatibility (every tool supports PNG)
No compatibility concerns for downloads or shared files
Better for files that will be opened in arbitrary software

a.en: Not supported in very old browsers (pre-2020 Safari)

b.en: No native animation (APNG is not widely supported)
Only lossless compression (no lossy option for photos)

a.en: Use WebP for web delivery where you control the serving infrastructure and can provide PNG fallbacks. Ideal when performance and file size are priorities and you are targeting modern browsers.

b.en: Use PNG when maximum software compatibility is needed — sharing files with clients, exporting for print, or any context where the file may be opened in arbitrary applications. PNG is also the safer choice for long-term archiving of graphics.

performanceNote.en: Google's benchmark shows WebP lossless images are 26% smaller than PNG on average. For a logo that is 50 KB as PNG, WebP lossless would be approximately 37 KB. For photographic images, WebP lossy achieves 25–35% smaller files than PNG at equivalent perceived quality.

verdict.en: For web delivery, WebP is the better choice — it produces smaller files while maintaining identical visual quality. Use PNG as a fallback for browsers that don't support WebP (now rare), and keep PNG as your master/source file since it has the best software compatibility for editing workflows.

question.en: Should I replace all PNGs on my website with WebP?

answer.en: For images served on the web, yes — WebP will improve your page load performance. Use the HTML picture element to serve WebP with PNG fallback: . Keep original PNGs in your source repository.

question.en: Is WebP lossless the same quality as PNG?

answer.en: Yes. WebP lossless compression is a different algorithm to PNG's DEFLATE, but both produce pixel-perfect lossless images. Decompressing a WebP lossless image gives you exactly the same pixels as the source — just like PNG, but typically at a smaller file size.
